narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 53.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning you placed in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average. This low performance stood out as the strongest signal to the model that bar exam success might be challenging. Counterbalancing that, your undergraduate GPA of 3.40 was a positive force, above the average of 3.21, helping pull the prediction toward passing and softening the overall risk. Your upper-middle family income also contributed favorably, as it aligns with backgrounds that often support stronger outcomes, compared to lower brackets more common in the dataset. Your LSAT score of 26, well below the average of about 37, and your full-time student status—shared by over 90% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. While these elements paint a mixed picture, the model weighed your weaker later-year grades most heavily against your strengths. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law, where perseverance often shines through systemic hurdles, but a data-driven call based on patterns in historical bar results. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score significantly, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying to schools with more holistic reviews. Gathering strong letters highlighting your resilience and any bar prep experience could also tip the scales next time. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this prediction in your favor.
